label: Cybernetics
definition: The interdisciplinary study of control, feedback, and communication in animals and machines, founded by Norbert Wiener in the 1940s. It was the intellectual precursor to artificial intelligence and the source of the feedback-loop thinking that pervades modern learning systems.

body.text: Cybernetics is the science of control and communication in the animal and the machine, the subtitle of the 1948 book by the mathematician Norbert Wiener that named the field. Its central object of study is the feedback loop: a system that senses the effect of its own actions and adjusts them to reach or maintain a goal, like a thermostat correcting toward a set temperature or an animal reaching for an object and refining the motion as it watches its hand. Wiener and his collaborators argued that this same circular, self-correcting logic governs steering engines, nervous systems, economies, and societies alike, and that it could be studied with one shared mathematical language of signals, control, and information.

Cybernetics matters to the history of Artificial Intelligence because it was the immediate intellectual precursor to the field, the dominant framework for thinking about intelligent machines in the decade before AI had a name. It grew from wartime work on automatic gun-laying, where a system had to track an aircraft and predict its motion, and it fused that engineering of control with new theories of information and computation and with neurophysiology. The 1943 model of McCulloch and Pitts, which treated idealized neurons as logical switches, was a cybernetic landmark, and the cross-disciplinary Macy conferences of the 1940s and 1950s gathered the mathematicians, engineers, psychologists, and biologists who built the field. When the Dartmouth Workshop coined "artificial intelligence" in 1956, it was in part marking out territory distinct from this older, more biologically minded tradition.

The two traditions diverged sharply over the following years, and the split shaped AI for decades. The new symbolic school that crystallized at Dartmouth located intelligence in the manipulation of explicit symbols and logical rules, a top-down program (later Symbolic AI) that bracketed the body and the control loop as irrelevant to thought. Cybernetics was bottom-up and embodied by contrast, treating intelligence as adaptive regulation that emerges from an agent coupled to its environment through continuous feedback. As symbolic AI captured the funding and the prestige, cybernetics faded as a named discipline through the 1960s and 1970s, its insights dispersing into control theory, robotics, systems biology, and the study of complex systems.

Cybernetics matters now because its core ideas returned, often without its name, at the heart of modern machine learning. The Perceptron and the whole Connectionist program descend directly from its neural modeling, so today's Neural Network is in a real sense a cybernetic artifact. Its feedback principle is the skeleton of reinforcement learning, where an agent acts, observes a reward, and adjusts, exactly Wiener's loop of action and correction; gradient descent is itself a feedback process that senses error and corrects parameters against it. The embodied, environment-coupled view that cybernetics championed also anticipated contemporary work in robotics and embodied cognition that treats intelligence as inseparable from a body acting in the world.

Cybernetics endures as the road not taken at AI's founding that turned out to be a road back. It supplied the vocabulary of feedback, control, and information that still organizes how engineers reason about adaptive systems, and it kept alive a view of intelligence as situated and self-regulating that the symbolic mainstream set aside and the learning era has since vindicated. Knowing the cybernetic lineage clarifies why so much of modern AI, from learning by error correction to agents shaped by their environments, looks less like the logic-and-search program of the Dartmouth founders and more like the feedback machines Wiener described first.
